abstract:
Modern video streaming technology has largely converged on a paradigm known as HTTP-based Adaptive Streaming (HAS). This approach offers two distinct advantages: by utilizing HTTP over TCP, it ensures seamless firewall/NAT traversal and fair bandwidth sharing; and by pre-encoding video at various discrete bitrates, it achieves scalable adaptivity without requiring real-time encoding during delivery. Traditionally, it has been assumed that the TCP throughput observed by a client serves as a reliable proxy for available network bandwidth, providing a stable reference for bitrate selection.

However, we argue that this assumption fails when HAS traffic constitutes a significant portion of network volume. Our research demonstrates that when multiple HAS clients compete for limited resources at a network bottleneck, the interplay between competing sessions and discrete bitrate levels prevents clients from accurately perceiving their fair share of bandwidth. Through theoretical analysis and empirical experiments, we show that this fundamental limitation causes significant video bitrate oscillations, which degrade the user experience. 

To address this, we propose that HAS requires an application-layer "probe-and-adapt" mechanism—one that functions similarly to, yet independently of, transport-layer TCP congestion control. We introduce PANDA, a client-side adaptation algorithm designed to operationalize this concept. Experimental results show that PANDA reduces bitrate instability by 60% compared to conventional methods while maintaining a consistent buffer safety margin.
